title: ABC-Bench: An Agentic Bio-Capabilities Benchmark for Biosecurity
abstract: Large language models are rapidly acquiring capabilities relevant to biological research. We introduce ABC-Bench, a suite of tasks to measure agentic biosecurity-relevant capabilities. All tested LLM agents outperformed the median expert human baseline on all three tasks: writing code to operate liquid handling robots, designing DNA fragments for in vitro assembly, and evading DNA synthesis screening.

title: Monte Carlo Pass Search: Using Trajectory Generation for 3D Counterfactual Pass Evaluation in Football
abstract: We introduce Monte Carlo Pass Search (MCPS), which infers kick parameters for each observed pass, samples execution variants and option variants, rolls each candidate forward with a ball-conditioned world model until the next ball interaction. Adapted a discrete-token autoregressive trajectory generator from autonomous driving.

title: CIAware-Bench: Benchmarking Control Intervention Awareness Across Frontier LLMs
abstract: AI control protocols oversee untrusted models by monitoring actions and modifying potentially unsafe steps. CIAware-Bench tests whether models can distinguish their own trajectories from those modified by a control intervention. Detection is generally easier across model families.

title: Superficial Beliefs in LLM Decision-Making
abstract: We ask whether LLMs merely imitate rationales when choosing between two options, or whether their choices reflect a systematic underlying decision structure. The resulting picture is neither one of arbitrary behaviour nor one of fully articulated belief - outputs are structured enough to support prediction, but explicit reasons track the recovered driver only imperfectly.

title: What Fits (Into Few Tokens) Doesn't Overfit: Compression and Generalization in ML Research Agents
abstract: Reusing a held-out benchmark adaptively should invite overfitting. Yet benchmark-driven ML has produced surprisingly little overfitting. We study this in the setting of LLM-driven research agents via two complementary information bottlenecks: output compression and input compression. Short prompts and compressible feedback are sufficient to reproduce high-performance models.

title: Workflow-GYM: Towards Long-Horizon Evaluation of Computer-use Agentic tasks in Real-World Professional Fields
abstract: We introduce Workflow-GYM, a benchmark for long-horizon GUI tasks centered on professional domains. Even the strongest models achieve only slightly above 30% success rates. Current agents struggle with workflow stage omission, error propagation, objective drift, and insufficient understanding of professional software environments.

title: The Role of Feedback Alignment in Self-Distillation
abstract: Investigation of feedback alignment mechanisms in knowledge distillation settings, analyzing how teacher-student signal propagation affects generalization.

title: TRACE: A Unified Rollout Budget Allocation Framework for Efficient Agentic Reinforcement Learning
abstract: This work targets multi-turn agentic RL by modeling each ReAct-style thought-action-observation turn as a semantically distinct node, allowing budget allocation to extend from prompt roots to turn-level prefixes with further continuations, which naturally forms tree-structured rollouts.
